name: Poem (Rust Web Framework with OpenAPI)

performance.notes: Built on hyper/tokio; competitive with Axum

agent_readiness.notes: Rust web framework with built-in OpenAPI via poem-openapi. use poem::{Route, Server, listener::TcpListener}. #[handler] async fn hello() -> String { 'Hello'.to_string() }. let app = Route::new().at('/', get(hello)); Server::new(TcpListener::bind('0.0.0.0:3000')).run(app).await. poem-openapi: use poem_openapi::{OpenApi, payload::Json, Object}. #[derive(Object)] struct User { id: i64, name: String }. struct Api; #[OpenApi] impl Api { #[oai(path='/user', method='get')] async fn get_user(&self) -> Json<User> }. let api_service = OpenApiService::new(Api, 'API', '1.0').server('http://localhost:3000'). Route with /openapi.json and Swagger UI built-in. WebSocket, SSE, multipart. Middleware tower-compatible.

gotchas: Less mainstream than Axum — smaller community
poem-openapi macro derive can have complex compile errors
Fewer Stack Overflow answers and tutorials
best_when: Rust APIs needing built-in OpenAPI/Swagger generation
When auto-generated API docs from code are required
Ergonomic async Rust APIs with derive macros
avoid_when: When community support and resources are critical (use Axum)
Teams unfamiliar with Rust derive macros
Maximum ecosystem compatibility (Axum's tower integration is broader)
summary: Poem is a Rust web framework with built-in OpenAPI via poem-openapi. #[OpenApi] impl Api { #[oai(path='/user', method='get')] async fn get() -> Json<T> }. Auto-generates /openapi.json and Swagger UI. Route + Server + TcpListener. WebSocket, SSE, middleware. MIT/Apache-2.0. Best Rust framework for built-in OpenAPI generation. Less community than Axum. Strong derive macro ergonomics.
